where_they_disagree: How many layers is too many (Alex: per-route + targeted; Sam: full layered system)
Whether 'high-risk' list is a layer or just an exception list
Initial framing - whether Activity changes the boundary strategy or augments it
context_dependent_answer: For a fintech app with compliance audit trail and white-screen incidents: per-route boundaries (Next.js error.tsx) + ~10 sub-boundaries for high-risk components + Activity-based graceful hide for non-critical surfaces + Sentry logging on every catch + sticky retry with backoff. For a CRUD admin tool: root + per-route is enough. For a complex IDE/builder (Figma-like) with extensive plugin surface: aggressive sub-boundaries (per-plugin, per-panel) because users tolerate widget failure better than full crashes. For a consumer media app: graceful hide dominates - users want to keep scrolling.

code_examples.approach: Layered error boundaries with Activity-based graceful hide
weaknesses: More complex than per-route only
Stale content requires cache infrastructure
Boundaries don't catch async errors in event handlers (need try/catch there)
No granular isolation
Whole feature dies on any internal error
No graceful degradation

decision_framework: debate-017: judge the error boundary strategy debate in this scenario: A fintech app needs an error handling strategy. Production has 3 user-visible white-screen-of-death incidents in the past month. Team must decide: one root error boundary, error bo. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors think error boundaries are 'about catching errors.' Senior insight: error boundaries are a UX policy decision - what does the user see when X fails? That decision varies by X. A failing payment form needs to surface clearly. A failing decorative widget should disappear. A failing chart should show last-good data. The 'one boundary' instinct conflates these. Also missed: silent failure is worse than visible failure when compliance is involved. Every catch must log, even if no UI changes.

scenario: A SaaS product with 12 main routes and ~80 components needs a code-splitting strategy. Current bundle is 480kb gzipped single chunk. Team debates: route-based splitting (one chunk per route), aggressive component-level splitting (lazy every modal/dialog/heavy widget), or 'don't split, just optimize the monolith.'
context: 7-engineer team, Vite + React Router 7. Average user visits 2-3 routes per session. ~30% of users see only the dashboard. Metrics: LCP 3.1s, TBT 580ms. Bundle analysis shows: 40kb of admin-only code (used by 4% of users), 70kb of charting library (used on 2 routes), 110kb of rich text editor (used in 1 route).
alex_initial_position: Component-level splitting on heavy widgets. Route-splitting alone doesn't help much because most users hit multiple routes - they pay the chunk-load cost on navigation. Splitting heavy components means the editor route loads its base chunk fast, then loads the editor when the user clicks 'edit.' That's the right granularity for the perceived perf win.
sam_initial_position: Route-level splitting first as table stakes - it's basically free with React Router 7. Then targeted component splits ONLY for components >50kb (the rich text editor at 110kb, the charts at 70kb). Don't split everything - the per-chunk overhead (HTTP request, parse, mount waterfall) accumulates and makes the app feel slower despite the lower TTI.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Route splitting alone gets you a 50% bundle reduction on initial load but a 200-400ms hitch on every navigation. Users perceive that hitch more than they perceive the initial load - they have an attention budget on first paint but not on click-to-next-page. Component splitting hides the hitch behind the click on the heavy component, which feels more natural.
debate.evidence: Web vitals: INP measures click-to-next-frame. A route navigation with a 300ms chunk load tanks INP. Suspense fallback during chunk load just makes it visible.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: You're describing the cost of naive route splitting. With React Router 7's loaders, you can preload the next route's chunk on hover/touch-start, so by the time the click registers, the chunk is in cache. Plus you can use the Service Worker to warm-cache likely-next routes after initial paint. The 'hitch' is solvable in user-time, not chunk-time.
debate.evidence: GitHub does exactly this: hover a repo link, the chunk is already downloading by the time you click. Click-to-navigate is <100ms.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Preloading on hover works for desktop but not mobile - no hover. Mobile users get the hitch unless we predict and prefetch, which is fragile. Component splitting doesn't need prediction - the user explicitly triggers the load by clicking 'open editor.'
debate.evidence: 38% of our users are mobile. Hover-based prefetch is desktop-only.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Mobile preloading: touchstart fires before click on tap, giving you ~100ms head start. Plus visibility-based preload (if a 'Edit' button is in viewport, preload editor chunk). The pattern works, just needs different triggers. And the alternative - component-level split with Suspense fallback - shows a loading state when the user clicks, which is also user-visible jank.
debate.evidence: <Suspense fallback={<Spinner/>}> still flashes a spinner. The user sees the load just as plainly as a route transition.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: A spinner inside a modal that's opening is expected. A spinner during page navigation is unexpected. There's a UX semantics difference: 'I clicked open this thing' tolerates a load; 'I clicked navigate to this page' expects instant. Component split fits user mental model better.
debate.evidence: Nielsen Norman: users tolerate 1-3s wait for explicit actions ('open editor') but expect <100ms for navigation.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Granted on the UX semantics. So: route splitting + intelligent preloading for routes (hover/visibility/touch), plus component splitting for the heavy widgets that match an explicit user action (editor, advanced filter dialog, video player). Combine, don't choose.
debate.evidence: This is what Next.js's link prefetching + dynamic imports gives you in the Next ecosystem. We replicate the pattern manually in Vite + React Router.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Right. And one more cut: admin code. 4% of users hit admin. That's a clear win for code splitting because it's a binary user segment - non-admin users never need that chunk. Bundle analysis-driven splitting: any chunk used by <10% of sessions becomes its own lazy chunk.
debate.evidence: Bundle delta from admin split: 40kb gzipped out of initial bundle. 96% of users get a 40kb smaller app.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Yes. And the chart library at 70kb across 2 routes - split it as a shared chunk that's loaded when either chart route loads. Don't inline it into both routes (duplicate cost) and don't put it in the main bundle (everyone pays).
debate.evidence: Vite's `manualChunks` config handles shared chunks. ~20 lines of config to express this.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Final plan: (1) route-based splitting via React Router 7 lazy routes; (2) explicit preload on hover/touchstart/visibility for likely-next routes; (3) component-level split for editor (110kb), charts (70kb, shared chunk for the 2 routes that use them), admin code (40kb, user-tier-gated); (4) main bundle target: <200kb gzipped.
debate.evidence: Total estimated reduction: 220kb gzipped from main bundle. New main bundle ~260kb, well within budget.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Plus instrumentation. Track chunk load times in production, alert on regressions. Without monitoring, code splitting can silently become slow as chunks bloat over time. A budget per chunk in CI prevents this.
debate.evidence: webpack-bundle-analyzer / vite-bundle-visualizer in CI. Fail PR if any chunk grows >20kb.

where_they_agree: Route splitting + preloading is table stakes
Component splitting for heavy widgets >50kb tied to explicit user actions
Admin/user-tier code splitting based on usage segments
Shared chunks for libraries used across multiple routes
Bundle budgets in CI to prevent regression
Naïve splitting is worse than no splitting - tooling matters
where_they_disagree: Whether hover-based preloading sufficiently solves the route-split hitch
Whether component-level splitting is preferable to aggressive route preloading
Initial granularity bias
context_dependent_answer: For a 12-route SaaS with mixed user segments: route splitting + smart preload + targeted component splits for heavy widgets (>50kb) + user-tier splits for admin code. Bundle budgets in CI. For a single-page app (no routes): component-level splitting only. For a marketing site (mostly read, mostly first-visit): route splitting + minimal preload, optimize main route's LCP. For a complex builder app (Figma-like) where one route is 80% of usage: split inside the main route by feature panel, not by route. For mobile-first apps: more aggressive splitting and touch-based preload, less reliance on hover.

strengths: Heavy widgets load on user intent
weaknesses: More config complexity
Preload logic must stay in sync with routes
Suspense fallbacks need designing for each surface
Network-poor users still hit chunk load delay
No within-route splits - heavy widgets load with page
Navigation hitch on every route change
Doesn't address charts shared across routes

decision_framework: debate-018: judge the code splitting philosophy debate in this scenario: A SaaS product with 12 main routes and ~80 components needs a code-splitting strategy. Current bundle is 480kb gzipped single chunk. Team debates: route-based splitting (one chunk . Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors split everything they can find a `lazy()` for. Senior insight: every chunk has overhead - HTTP request, parse, mount waterfall, Suspense fallback flash. The right number of chunks isn't 'as many as possible' but 'enough to match user-intent boundaries.' A 50-chunk app feels worse than a 5-chunk app even with smaller chunks, because the waterfall dominates. Also missed: code splitting without preloading is half the work. Preloading is what makes split chunks invisible to users.

scenario: A workflow automation app needs to implement a complex drag-and-drop canvas (think n8n / Zapier) where users connect nodes via lines. Accessibility audit revealed: drag-and-drop is unusable with keyboard, screen readers can't describe canvas state, and complex visual relationships aren't conveyed. Team debates: build a full alternative keyboard interface, use ARIA live regions to narrate the canvas, or accept that some users can't use this surface (provide a separate list-based editor).
context: 5-engineer team, product is used by SMB workflow builders. SOC2 compliance audit requires WCAG 2.2 AA. Sales team has lost 2 enterprise deals to a11y concerns. Canvas has up to 100 nodes per workflow.
alex_initial_position: Build a separate list-based editor that's accessible by default. The drag canvas is a specific UI affordance for visual thinkers; trying to make it equally usable for keyboard-only or screen-reader users is fighting the metaphor. Provide a parallel UI that does the same job differently - users pick based on need.
sam_initial_position: Make the canvas accessible. A separate list editor is segregation - 'accessible users get the worse experience.' WCAG's spirit is parity, not parallel implementations. Spend the engineering effort to make the canvas keyboard-navigable, screen-reader-narrated, and ARIA-described. Yes it's hard; it's what compliance and ethics require.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Canvas-with-keyboard-equivalent is a research project. You're proposing 'arrow keys move selection between nodes, enter selects, space initiates drag, arrow keys move during drag, escape cancels, tab cycles between node properties.' That's the API. Now: how does a screen reader user know which node is connected to which? Spatial relationships aren't readable. You'll fall back to describing them as a list of edges - which is just a list editor with extra steps.
debate.evidence: Excalidraw and tldraw both have keyboard nav for their canvases. Excalidraw's a11y is rated 'partial' by axe - screen reader users can't reasonably edit complex diagrams.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Partial isn't the same as impossible. The bar is 'can a screen reader user accomplish the core task' - which for a workflow builder is 'create a workflow, connect nodes, configure each node, save.' All of those are achievable with ARIA-live narration of state changes, keyboard nav between nodes, and a 'show connections' command that reads the edge list. The user doesn't need to see the spatial layout; they need to model the graph in their head from the audio description.
debate.evidence: GitHub Actions UI is canvas-like and is screen-reader-usable. Users navigate steps via keyboard, screen reader announces 'step 3 of 7, runs after step 2.' Same pattern applies.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: GitHub Actions is a linear pipeline, not a 2D graph with branching. Workflow builders have N-to-N connections, conditionals, parallel paths. The audio model for that is intrinsically more complex. Take 'what comes after this node' - in a list view, that's 'next item.' In a 2D graph, it might be 3 nodes, each labeled with a condition. Narrating that in keystroke-distance form is the list editor I proposed.
debate.evidence: WAI-ARIA APG: there's no recommended pattern for accessible 2D graph editing because it's an open research question.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Concession: full parity on the canvas is hard. Compromise: the canvas remains the primary editor for sighted/mouse users, but we provide an accessible 'graph view' mode (toggle button) that exposes the same data as a structured list with edit/connect/configure commands. Same data model, different UI. NOT a separate editor - the same workflow can be edited in either mode, switched freely.
debate.evidence: Figma offers 'layers' panel as the keyboard-accessible parallel to canvas. Both edit the same document. Acceptable to a11y reviewers.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: That's what I was proposing - 'list editor as alternative.' Calling it a 'mode' on the same data model is the right framing. Both edit the same workflow; users choose mode based on need. The mode is keyboard-first, screen-reader-narrated, no canvas needed.
debate.evidence: This is the standard 'two interfaces, one data model' pattern. Used by accessibility-conscious tools like Linear (keyboard-driven) where the visual is one of many ways to interact with the same data.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Agreed on the architecture. But the canvas itself still needs SOME keyboard support - users on temporary keyboard-only situations (broken mouse, RSI, etc.) need to use the canvas, not just the list mode. Minimum bar for canvas: keyboard nav between nodes, keyboard-triggered drag, focus indicators, and ARIA labels on nodes.
debate.evidence: WCAG 2.1.1 (Keyboard) is mandatory. All functionality must be operable via keyboard. The canvas as currently built fails this.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Right. Two-tier compliance: (1) canvas has full keyboard support (arrow keys, focus, drag via keyboard shortcuts) for situational keyboard users - meets WCAG 2.1.1; (2) list/structured mode provides full screen-reader-accessible editing - meets WCAG broader spirit. Sighted screen-reader users (low-vision) can use either mode.
debate.evidence: Two-tier accessibility is the standard for complex canvas-based tools (Figma, Miro, n8n).

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Plus the canvas needs ARIA-live announcements for state changes - 'node created,' 'connection made,' 'node A connected to node B.' Even if screen reader users primarily use list mode, the canvas should narrate for them when they're observing or collaborating.
debate.evidence: Live regions cost ~30 lines of code per state-change event. High ROI.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Add: focus management when canvas mutates. If a node is deleted, focus should move to a logical neighbor, not lose. If a connection is made, focus stays on the source node. These are details that matter for keyboard users.
debate.evidence: Focus management is the #1 a11y bug class for canvas-style editors per axe-core's lint rules.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Plan: (1) canvas keyboard support: focus, arrow nav, kbd-triggered drag, focus management on mutations; (2) ARIA-live narration of state changes; (3) parallel 'structured view' mode for full screen-reader workflow editing; (4) both modes edit same data model. Estimated effort: 4 engineer-weeks. Sales unblock: enterprise deals proceed.
debate.evidence: Two enterprise deals at $80k+ ARR each. Engineering cost ~$30k. ROI obvious.

where_they_agree: Full a11y parity on the canvas is not achievable - 2D spatial relationships are intrinsically hard to narrate
Parallel UI mode (structured view) is the right solution for screen-reader users
Canvas still needs keyboard support (WCAG 2.1.1 is mandatory)
ARIA-live regions narrate state changes
Two-mode editing on same data model > segregated editor
Focus management on mutations is mandatory
where_they_disagree: Whether 'parallel UI' is segregation or pragmatic - resolved when framed as 'mode on same data'
How achievable full canvas a11y is for graph editors
Where to draw the line between 'minimum WCAG compliance' and 'genuine equivalence'
context_dependent_answer: For a workflow builder with canvas + enterprise a11y requirements: dual-mode interface (canvas + structured view) on shared data model, keyboard support for the canvas, ARIA-live narration. Effort: ~4 engineer-weeks. For a drawing/whiteboard app (Excalidraw-like): canvas + alt-text export, structured view less critical because creative output is the goal. For a Figma-class design tool: full alternate panel with command palette (Figma does this). For a developer tool with code-as-source-of-truth: the code IS the accessible mode; canvas is a view.

strengths: Structured view provides full screen-reader workflow
weaknesses: Two UIs to maintain - feature parity discipline required
Power users need to learn both
Connections via keyboard in canvas is fiddly
Effort: ~4 weeks
Screen reader users still can't model 2D layout
Doesn't satisfy enterprise a11y review
Compliance theater

decision_framework: debate-019: judge the accessibility tradeoffs debate in this scenario: A workflow automation app needs to implement a complex drag-and-drop canvas (think n8n / Zapier) where users connect nodes via lines. Accessibility audit revealed: drag-and-drop is. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors think a11y is 'add some ARIA labels.' Senior insight: accessibility for visual/spatial UIs is an architecture decision, not a styling layer. If your UI fundamentally encodes meaning spatially (canvas, drag-drop, kanban), you cannot retrofit that into screen-reader semantics - you must build a parallel interface that exposes the same data structurally. The choice isn't 'should we be accessible' but 'do we have a data model that supports multiple views.' If yes, dual-mode works. If your spatial state IS your data model (e.g., x/y coordinates carry meaning), accessibility becomes hard and expensive.

scenario: A growing fintech (32 frontend engineers) is splitting into 5 product squads, each owning a vertical (checking, savings, cards, lending, investing). Current monolith Next.js app has gotten painful: deploy contention, slow CI (28 min), shared component library churn breaking 3 squads at a time. Team debates: keep monolith with stricter module boundaries, move to module federation (microfrontends), or adopt single-spa style runtime composition.
context: Each squad has 5-8 engineers. Shared concerns: auth, navigation, design system, analytics. Deploys per day: 12. ~600 components. Engineering wants squad autonomy; product wants shared UX consistency; security wants centralized auth. CTO is open but skeptical of microfrontends after a bad experience at a previous company.
alex_initial_position: Keep the monolith. Add strict module boundaries via ESLint package boundaries + a 'platform' shared package with auth/nav/design. Microfrontends solve organizational problems by creating technical complexity - you trade deploy contention for runtime integration bugs, version skew, and shared-dep nightmares. The CTO's bad experience is the median experience.
sam_initial_position: Module federation with squad-owned remote bundles. Each squad ships independently, can use their own React version if needed, and the platform team owns shared shells (auth, nav, design). Yes there's runtime complexity but it solves the actual organizational problem - 5 squads deploying through 1 pipeline is the root cause of every pain point.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Microfrontends solve deploy contention but introduce 4 new problems: (1) version skew between shared deps - React 18 in one squad, React 19 in another, hilarity ensues at runtime; (2) duplicated bundles - each remote ships its own React, total bundle 2-3x larger; (3) cross-squad navigation requires runtime contract between remotes; (4) error isolation - one squad's runtime error in their bundle can't easily be caught by another squad's boundary. These are why CTO's experience was bad.
debate.evidence: Spotify's microfrontend experience (publicly shared): they abandoned module federation in 2022 after 18 months due to exactly these issues. Reverted to monolith with strict boundaries.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Spotify's case was instructive but their team is 10x ours. At 32 engineers, the real cost is deploy contention - we measured 14% of weekly engineering time waiting for CI/CD slots and resolving merge conflicts on shared files. That's 4.5 engineer-weeks per week of waste. Module federation costs are real but bounded; deploy contention costs scale linearly with team size.
debate.evidence: Internal time-tracking: 14% on coordination overhead. At 5 squads x 6 engineers x 40 hours, that's $50k+/week.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: You're attacking deploy contention but the fix isn't microfrontends - it's better monorepo tooling. Turborepo + Vercel build caching + per-package change detection means a PR touching only the checking squad's code only triggers that squad's CI. Deploys per squad become independent within one monorepo. We get squad autonomy without runtime composition.
debate.evidence: Vercel's own dashboard is a Turborepo monorepo serving multiple product surfaces. They deploy 50+ times/day on a 200-engineer team.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Turborepo solves CI parallelism but not deploy autonomy. Even with Turborepo, all packages deploy as one Vercel app - they share the same build, the same deploy event, the same runtime. If the lending squad needs to roll back, every squad rolls back. Module federation gives each squad their own runtime artifact that deploys independently.
debate.evidence: Squad-independent rollback was a hard requirement from our incident review post-Q3 outage where one squad's bug forced cross-squad revert.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Squad-independent rollback is achievable in a monolith with feature flags. Each squad's code is behind a flag; rollback means flipping the flag, not rolling back the artifact. Faster than redeploying. And no runtime composition complexity.
debate.evidence: LaunchDarkly / Statsig flag-based rollback is sub-second vs minutes for redeploy.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Feature flags work for behavior changes, not for new code paths that depend on each other. If lending ships a new module that imports from a checking module that was reverted, your flag doesn't help - the build fails or the runtime error happens. You need physical separation, which means independent deploys.
debate.evidence: Coupling between squads is the issue. Flags reduce blast radius but don't break the deploy coupling.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Then enforce non-coupling. Lint rule: no cross-squad imports. Each squad only depends on `@platform/*` packages. If lending wants to use a checking thing, it goes through a platform-exposed API. That's the same boundary as microfrontends gives you, but without runtime integration cost.
debate.evidence: Linear uses this exact pattern - package boundaries enforced via ESLint, no cross-feature imports.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: That's defensible architecture but requires zero violation tolerance. Once one cross-squad import slips through, you have implicit coupling. With microfrontends, the boundary is physical - you literally can't import from another remote at build time. Discipline-based vs structural enforcement.
debate.evidence: Conway's law: org structure determines architecture. If we want squad autonomy, the architecture must enforce it, not rely on lint.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Real point. So tradeoff: monorepo with strict boundaries (lighter, discipline-based) vs module federation (heavier, structural). For 5 squads of 6 engineers, I lean monorepo + boundaries with serious lint enforcement and PR review. For 15 squads at 200 engineers, module federation becomes worth it. We're not at federation-scale yet.
debate.evidence: Common scale-up wisdom: monolith until ~50 engineers, then start splitting. We're at 32.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Concession: 32 engineers is below the federation breakeven. But we should architect TOWARDS federation - meaning: (1) Turborepo with strict per-squad packages now; (2) shared `@platform/*` packages own auth/nav/design; (3) lint-enforced no-cross-squad imports; (4) revisit federation when we hit 50 engineers or when deploy contention exceeds current pain. Don't federate today, but don't preclude it.
debate.evidence: Architectural runway pattern - design for the next 2x scale, not the current.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Agreed. Plus: invest in the platform team. The friction we're seeing in shared design system updates is because there's no team owning the platform package - it's a free-for-all. Dedicated 2-engineer platform team + governance process for changes prevents the 'breaking 3 squads at a time' issue.
debate.evidence: Stripe's platform team is 8 engineers serving ~150 product engineers. Roughly 5% of engineering on platform is the standard ratio.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Yes. Platform team owns: design system, auth/nav, shared utilities, CI/CD tooling, perf budgets. Product squads own their feature code only. Clear ownership = less stepping on toes.
debate.evidence: Platform engineering as a discipline. Established pattern.

where_they_agree: Module federation is over-engineering at 32 engineers - revisit at 50+
Strict package boundaries via ESLint, no cross-squad imports
Dedicated platform team owns shared concerns (auth, nav, design, tooling)
Turborepo for build parallelism + change detection
Feature flags for blast radius and rollback
Conway's Law: architecture must enforce desired team boundaries
where_they_disagree: Whether discipline-based boundaries are sufficient vs structural enforcement
How much to invest in 'federation runway' for future scale
Whether independent deploys are needed at current scale
context_dependent_answer: For 32 engineers in 5 squads: monorepo with Turborepo + strict ESLint package boundaries + dedicated 2-engineer platform team owning `@platform/*` packages. Architect for future federation but don't adopt it yet. For 100+ engineers in 10+ squads: module federation becomes viable. For <15 engineers: shared monolith, no formal boundaries needed. For polyglot teams (React + Vue + Angular squads): module federation is the only sane option. For tight UX consistency requirements: monolith dominates - federation makes nav/design coordination harder.

code_examples.approach: Turborepo monorepo with strict squad boundaries
strengths: Single React version, single bundle infra
Per-squad CI via Turborepo affected detection
Shared design + auth via platform packages
weaknesses: Boundary enforcement is discipline + lint, not structural
All squads deploy as one app
Coordinated rollback unless feature flags are diligent

code_examples.approach: Module federation (federation runway, when scale demands)
weaknesses: Version skew issues
Larger total bundle
Runtime composition bugs
Hardware/network failure modes are new

decision_framework: debate-020: judge the frontend architecture patterns debate in this scenario: A growing fintech (32 frontend engineers) is splitting into 5 product squads, each owning a vertical (checking, savings, cards, lending, investing). Current monolith Next.js app ha. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors think microfrontends are 'the next step.' Senior insight: microfrontends solve organizational scaling problems by introducing runtime composition problems. At the wrong scale, the cure is worse than the disease. Most companies that adopt microfrontends regret it at <100 engineers because the coordination problems they had were solvable with better monorepo tooling. Also missed: Conway's Law is real - your architecture will mirror your org structure whether you plan it or not. The right move is usually 'make the org boundaries explicit in package boundaries' not 'introduce runtime separation.'

scenario: An e-commerce checkout flow needs optimistic updates for: add-to-cart, update-quantity, apply-discount-code, remove-item. Current implementation is pessimistic (wait for server) and feels slow. Team debates: React Query's optimistic update API, the new React 19 useOptimistic hook + Server Actions, a custom 'pending intents' pattern with rollback, or 'just make the server faster.'
context: 4-engineer team, Next.js 15 app router. Median checkout API latency: 240ms p50, 800ms p99. 6% of optimistic updates revert (race condition with inventory). Cart abandonment is 28%; UX research traces 40% of abandons to 'felt slow.' Compliance: cart contents must never show a discount that the server rejected.
alex_initial_position: React 19 useOptimistic + Server Actions. Native React primitive, integrates with Suspense, handles pending state automatically, rollback is automatic on action failure. The 'compliance: never show invalid discount' is solved by useOptimistic's auto-revert on action throw. Don't roll your own; use the platform.
sam_initial_position: React Query's optimistic update with onMutate/onError. More mature, more flexible, better DevTools. useOptimistic is too new and has edge cases - what happens when 5 optimistic updates queue, then one fails? React Query has explicit semantics for this; useOptimistic's behavior is implementation-defined. We can't risk compliance bugs on bleeding-edge primitives.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: useOptimistic with Server Actions is the cleanest mental model: state is derived from the action chain, optimistic state shows the predicted result, real state replaces it when the action resolves. If action fails, optimistic state is dropped automatically. The 'race condition' you're worried about is the same in both approaches - the failure path is what differs, and useOptimistic's is simpler.
debate.evidence: useOptimistic's source: optimistic state is a derived layer over the actual state, replaced atomically. No partial state, no orphan optimistic updates.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: useOptimistic is bound to a transition - if you have 3 transitions in flight, the optimistic state is the latest one's prediction. That's fine for sequential updates, broken for concurrent. Add-to-cart and apply-discount happen concurrently in real users. With React Query, each mutation has its own onMutate and rollback; they don't trample each other. useOptimistic does.
debate.evidence: React 19 docs: useOptimistic 'shows optimistic state during a transition.' Plural transitions are not addressed.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: You can model concurrent operations as a single optimistic state shape - the cart is one entity, multiple operations queue as a list of pending intents, and the optimistic state is 'current cart + apply all pending intents.' That's a reducer pattern inside useOptimistic. Clean.
debate.evidence: useOptimistic's second argument is an action function that takes (currentState, optimisticValue) -> newState. You can implement a 'queue of intents' pattern inside it.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Sure, but at that point you've reimplemented React Query's mutation queue in 80 lines of code, with worse semantics (no per-mutation onError, no per-mutation retry, no DevTools inspection). The platform is offering you primitives, you're building a small library on top. Why not use the library that exists?
debate.evidence: React Query's mutation: ~50 lines of config gets you optimistic + rollback + retry + DevTools.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: React Query is great but it's 13kb gzipped and it's not aware of Next.js Server Actions - you'd skip Server Actions entirely and use it as a generic client mutation library. Server Actions have built-in CSRF protection, type-safe RPC, and integrate with Next's revalidation. Throwing them away to use React Query feels like fighting the framework.
debate.evidence: Next.js Server Actions auto-revalidate the route after mutations - data refetches without client refetch logic. React Query duplicates this work.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: You don't have to choose. Use Server Actions for the mutation transport (auth, validation, server work), and React Query for the client-side optimistic state and cache invalidation. Server Action returns the new cart state, React Query's onSuccess updates the cache. Best of both.
debate.evidence: This is a common Next.js 15 pattern: actions for mutation, React Query for cache. Vercel's own examples show this.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: That works for the mutation flow but useOptimistic still has a role for the OPTIMISTIC display state. Pattern: Server Action for the mutation, useOptimistic for the predicted UI during the action's in-flight period. React Query manages the cached state once the action resolves. Three roles, three layers.
debate.evidence: You can use all three - they're not mutually exclusive.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Three layers is over-engineered for our problem. Cart state is one entity; we don't need three caches representing it. Pick: useOptimistic + Server Action (no React Query) OR React Query + Server Action (no useOptimistic). I lean React Query because of the concurrent ops + compliance concerns - explicit rollback semantics are auditable.
debate.evidence: Audit story: 'show me the code that prevents an invalid discount from displaying.' React Query: onError -> rollback. Auditable. useOptimistic: implicit via action failure. Less auditable.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: That auditability point matters for compliance. Concession: React Query for cart state. Use Server Actions as the mutation transport, React Query for optimistic state + rollback + cache. useOptimistic skipped for this app. For other surfaces (single-shot updates like 'mark as read'), useOptimistic is fine.
debate.evidence: Differentiated tool use: cart needs explicit rollback (React Query), simple notifications can use useOptimistic.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Plus: server-side. 240ms p50, 800ms p99 is slow for an action - we should also profile and optimize. Optimistic updates hide latency but the 6% revert rate means we're lying to users sometimes. Fix the inventory check race condition too.
debate.evidence: 6% revert rate means 1-in-17 users sees a flicker. That's UX damage even with optimistic updates.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Right. Two parallel workstreams: (1) React Query + Server Actions for optimistic UX immediately; (2) backend perf + race condition fix in parallel. Don't pick one or the other.
debate.evidence: Frontend optimization and backend optimization compose.

where_they_agree: Server Actions as the mutation transport (CSRF, auth, revalidation built-in)
React Query for cart state due to explicit rollback semantics and audit trail
useOptimistic is fine for simple cases but not for concurrent operations on the same entity
Backend perf must improve in parallel - optimistic updates don't fix root cause
Concurrent optimistic operations need explicit queueing semantics
where_they_disagree: Whether useOptimistic alone is sufficient for cart (Sam: no due to concurrency; Alex: yes with intent queue)
Whether three layers (useOptimistic + RQ + Server Action) is over-engineered
Initial library bias for new code
context_dependent_answer: For e-commerce checkout with compliance constraints: React Query for cart state + Server Actions for mutation transport. Explicit onMutate/onError for auditable rollback. useOptimistic skipped for cart (concurrent ops), used elsewhere for simple cases. Parallel backend perf work. For a simple app with single-user single-entity mutations (e.g., 'mark as read'): useOptimistic alone is the right primitive. For a real-time collab app: Yjs handles optimistic + reconciliation natively. For a static-mostly site with rare mutations: pessimistic is fine.

code_examples.approach: React Query + Server Actions with optimistic + rollback
strengths: Handles concurrent mutations via React Query's queue
weaknesses: More config than useOptimistic
13kb React Query bundle
Two systems for state (RQ cache + RSC)
Concurrent mutations on same entity collide
No explicit retry
Less DevTools support
Rollback on throw - if action returns error tuple, no auto-rollback

decision_framework: debate-021: judge the data fetching strategy debate in this scenario: An e-commerce checkout flow needs optimistic updates for: add-to-cart, update-quantity, apply-discount-code, remove-item. Current implementation is pessimistic (wait for server) an. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors think optimistic updates are a 'feel-fast' feature. Senior insight: optimistic updates are a lie about state. Every lie has a rollback cost - when the lie is exposed (server rejects), the UI must revert. The right question is 'when the lie is exposed, what does the user see?' If rollback is silent and compliant (no stale data persisting), the lie is worth telling. If rollback can leave the UI in inconsistent state (e.g., showing a discount that no longer applies), the lie is dangerous. Also missed: not all updates should be optimistic. Payments shouldn't be - the user should see 'processing' clearly. Optimistic UX is a UX policy, not a perf trick.

scenario: A design system needs a Form Field composition API used across 40+ form types. Forms need: label, description, error state, helper text, required indicator, validation state styling, and tooltip help. Choosing between: a single <FormField> with many props, compound components (<FormField><FormField.Label/>...</FormField>), or a context-based API where consumers compose primitives freely.
context: Design system team, consumed by 5 product teams. Inconsistency complaints: 7 different ways labels relate to inputs (some use htmlFor, some don't, some wrap), 12 different error display patterns. a11y audit found 18 forms with broken label associations. Team has limited time to migrate existing forms.
alex_initial_position: Single <FormField> with props - label, description, error, required, helpText. Pass children for the input. Opinionated, prescriptive, consistent. The 7 ways of doing labels collapse to 1. Compound components push the consistency problem to consumers - they can still skip a Label, mis-order things, break a11y.
sam_initial_position: Compound components with a FormField context that auto-wires labels, ids, and ARIA. <FormField><FormField.Label>Email</FormField.Label><FormField.Input/><FormField.Error/></FormField>. The composition makes the structure visible, and the context handles the wiring. Designers prefer this because the JSX reads like the visual structure.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Compound components let consumers do things wrong. They can put <FormField.Error> BEFORE <FormField.Label>. They can omit <FormField.Label> entirely (still renders, no error, no a11y). With prop-based <FormField label='Email' error={...}>, the API doesn't allow these mistakes - label is required (TS error), error display is automatic, order is fixed.
debate.evidence: audit of 18 broken-label forms in current codebase: 14 of them used a compound pattern where someone forgot the Label.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Required label can be enforced in both APIs. <FormField label='...'> can require it; <FormField><FormField.Label>...</FormField.Label></FormField> can also require Label to exist via a runtime check or via TypeScript constraint on children. The pattern doesn't enforce - the API design does. And the prop-based version becomes unwieldy when you need conditional rendering: <FormField label={isAdvanced && 'Advanced'}> with a tooltip that only appears in some cases - lots of optional props sprawl.
debate.evidence: Compound design systems like Radix enforce structure via runtime checks (throw if FormField has no Label child). Type-safe via React.Children.toArray inspection.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Type-safe compound is theoretically possible but practically painful. TypeScript can't easily enforce 'this React.Children must include at least one child of type FormField.Label.' You can do it with branded types and a lot of ceremony. Easier: prop-based with `label: string` required. Simple constraint, no React.Children inspection needed.
debate.evidence: react-hook-form's FormField (in the shadcn/Radix style) uses runtime checks for label association. Works but adds dev-time errors that compile-time wouldn't have.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: You're optimizing the simple case. Real forms have complex variations: (1) split label-and-tooltip layouts; (2) suffix icons with help links; (3) conditional descriptions; (4) inline validation with custom error UI. Prop-based API ends up with 15 props plus 5 render-prop overrides (renderError, renderHelp, renderLabel). At that point you have all the complexity of compound with worse ergonomics.
debate.evidence: MUI's TextField has 35+ props. That's where the prop-based API leads when requirements grow.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Real point - prop APIs don't scale to many variants. Concession: compound components are right for the structural variety. But: the API needs strong defaults and forced association. Pattern: compound components with a FormField context that auto-generates id, auto-wires aria-describedby for description/error, throws (dev mode) if Label is missing. Structure is composable, associations are automatic.
debate.evidence: Adobe React Aria, Radix UI use this exact pattern.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Yes. Context auto-wires: useId() for input id, label htmlFor binding, aria-describedby pointing to description and error elements, aria-invalid driven by error state. Consumer composes structure, context handles a11y. Dev-mode warning if FormField renders without a Label child.
debate.evidence: useId is the modern way - id collision in SSR is solved.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Plus: provide a <FormField.Simple> sugar variant for the 80% case where you don't need composition flexibility. <FormField.Simple label='Email' error={err} required>{input}</FormField.Simple>. This is the prop-API I was advocating, but as ONE of the options in the compound system. Consumers pick complexity-vs-flexibility per field.
debate.evidence: Stripe's design system does this two-tier within FormField.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: FormField.Simple is the right escape hatch for trivial cases. Now: the migration problem. We have 5 product teams using current inconsistent forms. Big bang migration is risky; per-team migration creates a long period of mixed APIs. I'd suggest: ship the new API, write a codemod that converts the most common patterns (input + label + error) automatically, leave complex forms for manual migration.
debate.evidence: jscodeshift can convert simple cases in bulk. 80% automation, 20% manual.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Codemod yes. And: deprecate the old patterns in lint, fail CI on new code using them. The deprecation push prevents the migration from stalling indefinitely.
debate.evidence: Without forcing function, library migrations stretch to multiple years.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: One more constraint: react-hook-form integration. Most teams use RHF. Our FormField must accept RHF's register output cleanly. So the Input slot accepts ref + onChange + onBlur + name from RHF, and the error state flows from RHF's formState.errors. Document the integration as a first-class pattern.
debate.evidence: shadcn/ui's FormField is RHF-aware. We mirror that.

where_they_agree: Compound components with FormField context is the right primitive
Context auto-wires id, htmlFor, aria-describedby, aria-invalid
Dev-mode warnings for missing Label or unwired associations
FormField.Simple sugar for the 80% case
Codemod for migration + lint deprecation for forcing function
RHF integration as first-class
where_they_disagree: Where to draw the line between prop-based and compound
How much type-safety to enforce at compile-time vs runtime
context_dependent_answer: For a design system with 40+ form types across 5 teams: compound FormField with context-auto-wired a11y, plus FormField.Simple sugar variant, plus codemod for migration. Required dev-mode warnings. For a 5-form internal tool: single FormField with props - no need for composition flexibility. For an external component library (publishable): compound with extensive escape hatches - consumers will need flexibility you can't predict. For a forms-as-product app (Typeform-like): config-driven forms, FormField is internal infrastructure.

code_examples.approach: Compound FormField with context auto-wiring + Simple variant
weaknesses: Two APIs in one (Simple + Compound) - docs must explain both
React.Children inspection is fragile
Dev-mode warnings can be ignored
Context cost per field (minimal)
Doesn't scale - more variants = more props/render-prop sprawl
Hard to customize label layout
Locked structure

decision_framework: debate-022: judge the component composition patterns debate in this scenario: A design system needs a Form Field composition API used across 40+ form types. Forms need: label, description, error state, helper text, required indicator, validation state stylin. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors design FormField APIs based on what's pretty to write. Senior insight: FormField is an a11y enforcement mechanism disguised as a styling primitive. The job is to make it hard to write inaccessible forms. Auto-wiring htmlFor, aria-describedby, aria-invalid is the actual value - the visual styling is incidental. Also missed: form field APIs interact with form libraries (RHF, Formik). The 'right' API is the one that composes cleanly with your form library's register pattern - if you have to fight the integration, the API is wrong.

scenario: Choosing between Jotai and Recoil-style atomic state for a complex spreadsheet-like app where individual cells have computed dependencies on other cells (formulas), and cell changes need to trigger minimal re-renders of dependent cells only. Also considering signals (Preact Signals / TC39 proposal) and a hand-rolled dependency graph.
context: 4-engineer team building a budgeting tool with 1,000+ cells per sheet, each potentially formula-bound. Performance target: typing in a cell updates dependent cells within one frame (16ms). Current React state in a single useReducer is rebuilding the entire grid on each keystroke - measured at 240ms per update.
alex_initial_position: Preact Signals (or the TC39 Signals proposal via polyfill). Signals are designed for exactly this: fine-grained reactivity where dependents auto-track and only re-render on actual change. Jotai's atom graph works but has React-reconciler overhead that signals bypass. Best perf for the cell-grid problem.
sam_initial_position: Jotai. The atom-with-dependencies pattern is mature, has React-integrated devtools, and handles SSR + Suspense cleanly. Signals are conceptually similar but the React integration (signals-react-runtime) is experimental and the reactivity model conflicts with React's render model. Jotai is the production-ready choice; signals are the bleeding-edge gamble.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Jotai's atom dependency graph is fine for moderate dependencies, but for 1,000 cells with potentially 1,000 formulas, the React reconciliation overhead is the bottleneck. Each cell that subscribes via useAtom is a React subscription, which goes through useSyncExternalStore, which schedules a render. With signals, the cell reads `cellValue.value` directly, the runtime tracks the dependency, and updates happen via fine-grained DOM patches - bypassing React reconciliation entirely for the cell value.
debate.evidence: Preact Signals benchmarks: 10,000 reactive nodes updating at 60fps. React's reconciler can't match this on the same hardware.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Preact Signals in Preact, sure. In React 19 with the signals-react-runtime, you're either (a) running them outside React's render (DOM patches via the signal runtime, breaking React's mental model), or (b) wrapping them in useSyncExternalStore for each cell, which is exactly Jotai's overhead. The 'bypass React' magic is fragile - it breaks SSR, Suspense, useTransition, and any component that needs to read a signal value in JSX.
debate.evidence: signals-react-runtime requires a Babel transform that's not in stable React. Production-readiness is unclear.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Concession: the React integration story for signals is rough. But Jotai has its own perf wall at scale. Atom dependencies in Jotai are computed via a graph the library maintains; when you have a derived atom that depends on 200 source atoms, every source atom change triggers a recomputation traversal. That gets expensive at our scale.
debate.evidence: Jotai's source: getAtom calls trigger upstream traversal. O(deps) per atom read.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: O(deps) is unavoidable for any reactive system - signals do the same traversal, they just call it 'dirty propagation' instead. The difference isn't algorithmic, it's React-overhead per subscription. With Jotai, each cell uses useAtom -> useSyncExternalStore -> selector. That's maybe 0.1ms per cell on a re-render. 1,000 cells, if all need to re-render on a formula change, is 100ms. Not great but not catastrophic.
debate.evidence: Jotai's overhead per subscription: profiled at 80-120us. 1,000 cells = 80-120ms total React overhead.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: 100ms is exactly our 16ms budget violated by 6x. We need fine-grained DOM updates, not React re-renders, for the cells. Signal-based runtime updates the cell's text node directly without going through React's reconciler. THAT's how we hit 16ms.
debate.evidence: Preact signals direct DOM update: ~5us per update. 1,000 cells changing = 5ms total.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Counter: most formula changes don't affect 1,000 cells. They affect maybe 10-50 dependents. So the realistic re-render scope is small. With Jotai's selector pattern (useAtom with a select), only the cells whose value actually changed re-render. The 100ms estimate is worst-case.
debate.evidence: Spreadsheet typing: each keystroke affects N dependent cells where N is typically <20 in real spreadsheets.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Real point. So the perf-tier question is: do we have the rare big-update case (recalc entire sheet) where 1,000 cells update? If yes, signals win. If no, Jotai is fine. Our app has 'recalc on file load' and 'recalc on column-wide formula change' which CAN hit hundreds of cells. We need to handle both cases.
debate.evidence: User flow: paste a formula into 200 cells - triggers 200 dependent recalcs in many cells. Common enough to design for.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Hybrid approach: Jotai for normal interactivity, batched updates with `unstable_batchedUpdates` (or React 19's automatic batching) for bulk operations. Or: detect bulk-update scenarios and switch to a non-React rendering path for those moments. Don't change the architecture for the 1% case.
debate.evidence: React 19's automatic batching handles many cases. Plus Jotai has its own batching primitives.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Batching helps but doesn't change the per-cell render cost - it just merges them into one render pass. 200 cells re-rendering in one pass is still 16-24ms of React work. Signals avoid React work entirely.
debate.evidence: Render batching reduces overhead by ~30%, not the order of magnitude needed for 1,000+ cells.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Final pragmatic position: build with Jotai, instrument perf carefully, identify the actual hot paths in production usage. If sheets routinely exceed 500 cells with deep dependency chains, evaluate signals or a custom virtualized renderer with non-React updates for the grid surface. Don't bet the architecture on speculative perf needs.
debate.evidence: Premature signal-isation has the same risk as premature optimization.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Concession: Jotai is the safer 80% answer. But: design the cell rendering so swapping in signals later is feasible - keep the cell as a pure subscribe-and-render component, not entangled with parent state. If/when we need signals, the migration is contained.
debate.evidence: Architectural reversibility - design for swap, don't bet on one runtime.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Yes. And: use virtual scrolling for the grid (react-window or similar) so only visible cells render. With 1,000 cells in the data model but only ~80 rendered at any time, the actual React work is small.
debate.evidence: Visible cell count is bounded by viewport, not by data size. Virtualization is mandatory at this scale regardless of state library.

where_they_agree: Virtual scrolling for the grid - only render visible cells
Jotai is production-ready; signals are experimental in React
Most formula changes affect <20 dependents, not 1,000
Design cells as isolated subscribers so state lib swap is feasible
Bulk-update scenarios may require non-React rendering paths
where_they_disagree: Whether the rare 200+ cell update case justifies signals from day one
How much architectural risk signals introduce in React 19
Whether Jotai's React-subscription overhead is acceptable at scale
context_dependent_answer: For a 1,000-cell spreadsheet app: start with Jotai + virtualization + careful cell isolation. Instrument perf, identify hot paths in real usage, consider signals only if measured perf misses budget on common operations. For a 10,000+ cell sheet (Excel-class): build a custom renderer with non-React updates for the grid surface; React owns the chrome. For a moderate-state app (forms, dashboards): pure React state or Zustand - this debate doesn't apply. For a graph editor with reactive node values: signals fit the model conceptually but production readiness in React is still maturing.

code_examples.approach: Jotai with atom families for cells + virtualization
strengths: Each cell subscribes only to its own atom + deps
weaknesses: Per-cell React subscription cost (~100us)
Bulk updates batch but still hit React reconciler
Atom-family memory grows with cell count

strengths: No React reconciliation for cell value updates
weaknesses: Experimental React runtime
Breaks SSR, Suspense semantics
Signal updates outside React render are mental-model violation
Less mature DevTools

decision_framework: debate-023: judge the state management architecture debate in this scenario: Choosing between Jotai and Recoil-style atomic state for a complex spreadsheet-like app where individual cells have computed dependencies on other cells (formulas), and cell change. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors think reactive state is reactive state. Senior insight: in React, every reactive primitive has to interface with React's render model, and that interface (useSyncExternalStore, useState) has overhead. Signals can bypass it but at the cost of being outside React. Jotai works within it but pays the overhead. There's no free lunch - you pick where you pay. Also missed: for spreadsheet-like apps, the state library matters less than the rendering strategy. Virtualization is mandatory at scale regardless of which state lib you choose.

scenario: A team must decide whether to enable React Compiler (formerly Forget) on a 200k LOC codebase. Pros: auto-memoization eliminates manual useCallback/useMemo. Cons: compile-time cost adds 25s to dev builds, runtime overhead from generated memoization wrappers, some edge cases break (mutable refs, non-pure render functions). The team is split.
context: 6-engineer team, React 19, Vite. Codebase has ~3,400 useCallback and useMemo calls accumulated over 3 years. Bundle includes ~18kb of memoization-related code. Some components are demonstrably non-pure (set state during render with conditions, mutable refs as deps). Lint config currently disallows React Compiler's strict rules.
alex_initial_position: Enable it. The compiler removes the cognitive overhead of when-to-memoize, which is the #1 source of perf regressions in our codebase. The 25s dev build hit is annoying but tolerable. Edge cases will surface during enablement - fix them with the eslint-plugin-react-compiler's rules. Trade dev velocity for correctness.
sam_initial_position: Don't enable globally yet. Enable opt-in per directory via the compiler's directive. The codebase has accumulated impurity over 3 years - 'set state during render' patterns we don't even know exist. Auto-memoizing impure code creates subtle bugs that look like 'this component sometimes doesn't re-render.' Better to clean up impurity first, then enable.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Per-directory opt-in creates the same boundary problem as per-file strict TS - the strictness leaks. A non-compiled file imports a compiled file's exports, the contract between them is ambiguous. Better: enable globally, run the React Compiler lint rules in 'warn' mode for one sprint to surface impurity, fix it as it's found, then 'error' mode.
debate.evidence: The compiler's eslint plugin catches impurity at lint time, not at compile time. We can surface the issues without enabling the compiler's runtime first.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Lint surface is real - and that's the order I'd advocate too. Lint first (catch impurity), fix issues, then enable compiler. But you said 'enable globally + warn lint': that means the compiler IS running and silently miscompiling impure components in prod. We don't have a way to know which components are silently broken.
debate.evidence: React Compiler's docs: impure components are 'best effort' compiled. The semantics for impure-but-compiled code are 'undefined behavior.'

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: OK that's a real concern. Revised order: (1) enable eslint-plugin-react-compiler in error mode, fix all violations; (2) then enable the compiler. Two phases.
debate.evidence: This is the official Meta migration playbook for the compiler.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Phase 1's scope is 3,400 useCallback/useMemo calls plus an unknown number of impurity violations. Estimate: 2-3 engineer-weeks of fix work. The 'auto-memoization' win has to justify that. What's the actual measured perf improvement on our app?
debate.evidence: Meta's reported gains from Compiler: 15-30% in re-render reduction. But that's an average; some apps see <5%.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Measure first. Build a perf-test corpus: 10 representative interactions (open modal, type in form, scroll feed, etc.). Run with compiler off vs compiler on (without fixing impurity yet - just to see the upper bound). If compiler shows >10% improvement on the perf corpus, do the 2-3 week migration. If <10%, skip.
debate.evidence: Empirical decision-making. Don't migrate based on industry hype.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Agreed. And: a secondary measurement - dev build time. We're already at 8s cold dev start with Vite. Compiler adds 25s. That's a 4x dev-velocity regression that hits every engineer all day. Worth quantifying: 25s x 30 cold starts/day x 6 engineers = 75 minutes/day. Not trivial.
debate.evidence: Cold start frequency: per engineer, ~10-30 times/day based on team's measured habits.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Compiler runs incrementally - only files that changed get recompiled. HMR cost is sub-second. The 25s is cold-start only, which is 1-3 times/day for most engineers. Multi-minute daily cost is overstated.
debate.evidence: React Compiler caches AST analysis. HMR with compiler is profiled at 200-400ms.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Granted. Cold-start hit is the meaningful one. So decision tree: measure perf benefit, measure dev cost honestly (cold-start only), do migration if perf benefit justifies fix-cost + ongoing dev cost.
debate.evidence: Decision criteria explicit upfront, not retrofitted.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: One more thing: bundle. Compiler-generated memoization replaces our manual useMemo/useCallback usage. If the compiler is more efficient, bundle drops. If less, bundle grows. Worth measuring.
debate.evidence: Compiler-generated memoization is typically more compact than hand-written useMemo - the compiler can inline equality checks.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: And one we haven't discussed: the legacy useCallback/useMemo calls. After compiler enabled, those become redundant. Do we leave them (no perf cost, but visual noise) or strip them (codemod, but adds migration risk)? I'd leave them and let new code be useCallback-free. Stripping is a separate cleanup project.
debate.evidence: Stripping useCallback en masse has historically caused regressions because some useCallback usages were also acting as ref-stable functions for non-memoization purposes (event handlers passed to imperative APIs).

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Right. Don't strip. New code goes compiler-only; legacy keeps its memoization. Over time, refactors organically replace them.
debate.evidence: Brownfield migration discipline: never refactor for the sake of refactoring.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Final plan: (1) measure perf benefit on 10-interaction corpus; (2) if >10% benefit, enable eslint-plugin-react-compiler in error mode and fix violations over 2-3 weeks; (3) enable compiler globally; (4) leave legacy memoization in place; (5) monitor cold-start regression and mitigate if >40s.
debate.evidence: Phased, measurable, reversible.

where_they_agree: Measure perf benefit on representative corpus before deciding
Lint plugin in error mode first, compiler enabled second
Per-directory opt-in is problematic - boundary leakage
Leave legacy useCallback/useMemo in place
HMR cost with compiler is acceptable; cold-start is the real dev tax
Compiler is a productivity tool, not a panacea
where_they_disagree: Whether the 2-3 week impurity cleanup is worth doing
How seriously to weigh dev build time regression
Risk tolerance for compiler edge cases in prod
context_dependent_answer: For a 3-year-old 200k LOC codebase: measure first, fix impurity via lint, then enable globally. Reversible. For a greenfield project: enable from day one, write compiler-clean code from the start. For a legacy codebase with extensive class components or non-pure patterns: skip - cost of cleanup exceeds compiler benefit. For perf-critical apps already manually optimized: marginal benefit; cost may not justify. For mid-sized apps with naive memoization (heavy useCallback culture): high ROI - compiler does what engineers were doing manually but better.

strengths: Lint catches impurity before compiler runs
Legacy code unchanged - low migration risk
weaknesses: 2-3 week fix effort
Cold-start dev cost +25s
Some edge cases unfixable without significant refactor
Impurity bugs land in prod silently
Cold dev startup hit immediately
Risk of regression on undetected edge cases

decision_framework: debate-024: judge the performance vs dx tradeoffs debate in this scenario: A team must decide whether to enable React Compiler (formerly Forget) on a 200k LOC codebase. Pros: auto-memoization eliminates manual useCallback/useMemo. Cons: compile-time cost . Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors enable the new shiny thing because it sounds good. Senior insight: compilers that transform your code have semantic implications. The React Compiler assumes purity; non-pure code becomes undefined behavior. In a 3-year-old codebase, impurity is everywhere and undocumented. Enabling the compiler without auditing creates ghost bugs - components that 'sometimes don't re-render' with no obvious cause. Also missed: dev tooling cost is engineering cost. A 25s cold-start hit applied 30 times a day across 6 engineers is real money. Migrations have to justify both the runtime AND tooling cost.

scenario: A B2B SaaS team is deciding between client-side rendering (CSR) with a JSON API, server-side rendering with React Server Components (RSC), and a hybrid (RSC for the shell + CSR for the interactive surfaces). The product is a complex IDE-like editor with heavy client interactivity but also marketing pages, settings pages, and dashboards that are mostly read-only.
context: 5-engineer team, Next.js 15 available. SEO matters for marketing pages but not for the IDE surface. Time-to-interactive on the IDE is the dominant perf metric (currently 3.4s). Current is fully CSR with a JSON API. Some pages have measurably bad LCP (settings: 2.8s, dashboard: 2.1s). Compliance: data must not be in initial HTML (some screens show PII).
alex_initial_position: Stay CSR for the IDE, add RSC only for marketing pages (separate Next.js app or dedicated route segment). RSC's appeal is for read-heavy SEO-relevant pages. The IDE is neither - it's interactive and authenticated. Adding RSC to the IDE just adds complexity without payoff. Marketing pages are the right use case.
sam_initial_position: Use RSC pervasively for the shell of every page (settings, dashboard, IDE) - it ships zero JS for the static structure. Client components for the interactive bits. This gives us fast LCP via streamed HTML for the chrome, then the interactive surface boots over it. PII compliance is handled via 'use client' boundaries that don't accept PII as props.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: RSC is great in theory but it's leaky in practice. 'Server component renders user list, passes data to client component' - now you have to think about which props serialize across the boundary, what's a Server Action vs an API call, and Suspense waterfalls that bite you because the next streamed chunk depends on the previous. The mental model overhead is significant. CSR has one model: client makes API calls.
debate.evidence: Vercel's own blog posts about RSC migration include sections like 'gotchas with Suspense boundaries.' Production-readiness is real but rough.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: The mental model overhead is real but it pays for itself in TTI. Settings page with CSR: fetch user, fetch teams, fetch billing, fetch preferences - 4 sequential round trips on initial load. Settings page with RSC: server fetches all 4 in parallel, streams HTML, total wall time is max of 4 not sum. Measured TTI difference on dashboard-style pages: 1.5-2x faster.
debate.evidence: https://web.dev/articles/rsc-perf - Vercel measured 1.5-2x TTI improvement on dashboard-class pages with RSC vs CSR.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: That's for dashboard pages that are read-mostly. The IDE doesn't benefit - it's interactive. Once the IDE loads, every interaction is client-side anyway. Server rendering the shell adds streamed HTML but the JS still has to download and hydrate to make it interactive. You're optimizing first paint at the cost of first interaction.
debate.evidence: For interactive surfaces: hydration cost is 200-400ms even with streaming. CSR's blank-screen-then-render is paradoxically faster to TTI for some apps.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Hydration cost is real but it's bounded by component count. For the IDE shell (nav, breadcrumbs, toolbar) it's small. The editor itself is a client component that boots independently after hydration of the shell. User sees the shell instantly, the editor lights up shortly after. That sequence feels better than 'blank screen for 3s.'
debate.evidence: Linear's app uses a server-rendered shell + client editor pattern. Users perceive faster startup.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: PII concern: the compliance requirement says no PII in initial HTML. If settings page is RSC, the user's email/name/etc. is in the server-rendered HTML. Sent over the wire as plaintext. Cached by intermediate proxies if any caching layer doesn't respect Cache-Control. That's a compliance risk our security team won't accept.
debate.evidence: Specific compliance requirement: 'PII must only be loaded post-authentication via authenticated API calls.' HTML in document body fails this.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: PII in RSC HTML can be addressed: use Cache-Control: private + no caching headers, or use client components for PII-bearing UI (server renders the shell, client fetches PII). The mental model is 'server renders structure, client fetches sensitive data.' That's a clean separation.
debate.evidence: Stripe Dashboard does this: server-rendered nav and chrome, client-fetched account data. PII never in initial HTML.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Then the win shrinks. If PII has to be client-fetched, the 'no waterfalls' benefit of RSC doesn't apply for screens that primarily display PII. Settings page is mostly PII. Dashboard widgets often are. The pages where RSC shines (marketing, public docs) we agree on; the pages where the win might apply (settings, dashboard) might still need client fetches for compliance.
debate.evidence: PII-heavy pages: 60% of dashboard widgets contain user-specific data.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Right. So the actual sweet spot for RSC in this app is: (1) marketing pages - full RSC; (2) page shells (nav, header, footer) - RSC; (3) read-only data that's non-PII (recent activity feed metadata, public team info) - RSC; (4) PII-bearing widgets - client components with API calls; (5) interactive surfaces (IDE editor) - client components. Per-component decision based on data sensitivity and interactivity.
debate.evidence: This is the 'islands' pattern - server renders chrome, client islands handle interactive/sensitive.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Islands approach I can defend. Concrete rules: (1) any component that needs PII -> 'use client' + API call; (2) any component that's interactive beyond basic links -> 'use client'; (3) chrome/shell/non-PII content -> server component. The PII rule is the compliance enforcement.
debate.evidence: Three rules, no ambiguity. Engineers can apply consistently.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Plus: a lint rule that flags PII-typed props on server components. We define a TS marker type `Sensitive<T>` for PII data, and a lint rule that prevents `Sensitive<T>` from being passed to server components.
debate.evidence: Type-driven compliance enforcement. Catches violations at PR time.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: That works. Migration plan: (1) marketing routes -> RSC immediately (Next.js app router); (2) page shells (auth wrapper, nav) -> RSC by default; (3) interactive surfaces (IDE) -> keep CSR; (4) data widgets -> evaluate per-widget for PII; (5) lint enforcement for the Sensitive type. Measure TTI before and after on each migrated surface.
debate.evidence: Phased migration with measurement, lint-enforced compliance, clear rules. Estimated 4 sprint cycles.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Yes. Plus: don't break the IDE. The current IDE works - any RSC integration there is opt-in and measured. We're not refactoring a working surface for theoretical wins.
debate.evidence: 'If it works, don't unship it' applies to architecture migrations.

where_they_agree: Marketing pages: full RSC - clear win, no PII concerns
Page shells (nav, chrome, footer): RSC for fast LCP
Interactive surfaces (IDE): keep CSR - hydration cost > render benefit
PII-bearing widgets: client components with authenticated API calls
Lint enforcement via TS marker types for PII compliance
Per-component decision, not per-page or per-app
where_they_disagree: How much of the dashboard/settings can use RSC vs PII rule pushing them to client
Whether RSC's mental model overhead is worth the TTI win
Order and pace of migration
context_dependent_answer: For a complex B2B SaaS with mix of interactive + read pages + PII compliance: islands pattern - RSC for shell and non-PII content, client components for PII and interactive surfaces. Lint-enforced PII type guards. Marketing pages full RSC. IDE stays CSR. For a content site (blog, docs): full RSC, easy win. For a single-page IDE/builder (Figma-like): pure CSR, RSC adds complexity without benefit. For a marketing-driven B2C app: RSC by default, client components only for interactivity.

code_examples.approach: Islands: RSC shell + client components for interactive/PII
strengths: PII compliance via client fetches with auth
weaknesses: Multiple mental models per page
Lint rule for PII boundary requires implementation
Server/client boundary requires team education

code_examples.approach: Full CSR with API gateway (status quo, sometimes correct)
weaknesses: Sequential fetch waterfalls hurt TTI
Blank screen on slow connections
SEO impossible without separate SSG path

decision_framework: debate-025: judge the frontend architecture patterns debate in this scenario: A B2B SaaS team is deciding between client-side rendering (CSR) with a JSON API, server-side rendering with React Server Components (RSC), and a hybrid (RSC for the shell + CSR for. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors think RSC is 'just like SSR but better.' Senior insight: RSC has a new data boundary (server -> client serialization) that creates new design constraints. PII compliance, props serialization, Suspense waterfalls, Server Action vs API call - these are real decisions on every component boundary. The mental model overhead is non-trivial. Also missed: RSC is great for some pages, not all. The 'use it everywhere' instinct is wrong - interactive surfaces don't benefit (hydration cost exceeds streaming gain). Per-page decision, not per-app.
